title: Ginger infusion for nausea
subtitle: The kitchen's oldest anti-nausea remedy
excerpt: A simple hot infusion of fresh ginger root. Traditionally taken for nausea from travel, morning sickness at low dose, or post-meal queasiness. One of the most studied herbs in modern herbal medicine.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), ginger entry, anti-nausea tradition, culinary-medicinal use. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), ginger as an antiemetic and circulatory herb. WHO Monograph on Zingiber officinale (1999), evidence summary including limited clinical evidence for nausea and morning sickness. Cochrane review: Boone and Shields (2005), 'Treating pregnancy-related nausea and vomiting with ginger', limited clinical trial evidence for low-dose use in early pregnancy.

content.text: A cup of
ginger infusion
for nausea. Fresh ginger root, sliced thin and steeped covered in hot water for ten minutes,
antiemetic
and the kitchen's first response to travel sickness, queasiness after a rich meal, or an unsettled stomach in cold weather. A long-standing kitchen tradition for settling the gut.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Blood thinners: ginger can thin the blood a little at daily working doses. If you take warfarin, aspirin, or other blood thinners, keep ginger to cooking levels and check with the prescriber before going beyond one or two strong cups a day.

Pregnancy: a low dose of ginger (up to about 1 g of dried-ginger strength a day, near a cup of medium-strength fresh ginger tea) is widely supported for morning sickness in the first three months. Bigger doses (more than 2 g of dried-ginger strength a day, or strong daily tinctures) are less well studied and best discussed with a midwife or herbalist. Cooking use is fine.

Children: paediatric use is not adult-divided-by-half. Talk to a herbalist or GP before using ginger preparations in children under twelve.

Seek medical care for nausea with a bad headache, stiff neck, severe belly pain, blood in vomit, signs of dehydration, or nausea that lasts more than two days without a clear cause.
attrs.title: Medical notes

content.text: What you need

items.name: Ginger
items.prepNote: fresh, unpeeled, about a 2 cm piece, sliced into 4, 6 thin rounds

content.text: The standard cup is about 10 g fresh ginger (a 2 cm piece) in 250 ml of water. That gives about 1 g of dried-ginger strength. It is the lower end of the working dose, the level long-noted as safe for short bouts of nausea.
Method
Slice the ginger

Cut {{ginger-root}} of fresh ginger into thin rounds, about 3 mm each, no need to peel, the peel adds body to the flavour. The finer the slice, the more surface area exposed to the water and the more robust the cup.
Steep covered, 10 minutes

Place the ginger slices in a teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and put the lid on immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. The cup will be golden and smell strongly of ginger when it is ready. Longer steeping (up to 15 minutes) produces a stronger, slightly more peppery cup, which some readers prefer.
Strain and sip slowly

Strain the slices out. Sip the cup slowly, for nausea, sipping rather than drinking quickly gives the active constituents time to work without adding more liquid to a struggling stomach. A small amount of honey softens the heat of the ginger if the spice is too much.
Dosing

One cup at the onset of nausea. Up to three cups a day during an acute episode. The traditional course is short, a cup or two until the nausea settles. For travel sickness, drinking a cup 30 minutes before the journey begins is the traditional approach. For morning sickness, the first cup of the day before rising, kept at the bedside, follows the traditional pattern. If nausea persists beyond 48 hours, see your doctor.

Children under twelve: not recommended without paediatric advice. For school-aged children, a half-strength cup (5 g ginger per 250 ml, steeped 5 minutes) is within the traditional range; always check with the GP first if nausea is frequent.
Storage

Fresh ginger root keeps 3 weeks loosely wrapped in the fridge, or slice and freeze in a bag for up to 6 months. Frozen slices can go directly into the teapot without defrosting, add 2 minutes to the steep time. Brewed ginger tea does not keep; make each cup fresh.
When NOT to use this remedy

Seek medical care for nausea with a bad headache or stiff neck. Or with severe belly pain. Or with blood in the vomit. Or with signs of dehydration (very dark urine, no urination for hours, dizzy on standing). Seek medical care for nausea that lasts more than two days without a clear cause. Seek medical care for sudden nausea in someone with a known heart condition.

Skip daily strong doses (more than two strong cups a day) if you take blood thinners, unless the prescriber agrees. For morning sickness past the first three months, lasting nausea in pregnancy, or any pregnancy worry, the cup of ginger tea sits alongside midwife or obstetric care, not in place of it.

attrs.intro: Two common reasons ginger tea disappoints for nausea.

items.fix: Fresh ginger should feel firm and smell spicy when cut. Use a 2 cm piece per 250 ml cup minimum. Thin slicing exposes more surface area.
items.cause: Old ginger with low gingerol content, or too little ginger.
items.symptom: Cup is weak and not noticeably spicy

items.fix: Let the cup cool to warm rather than hot before sipping. Sip in small amounts over 20, 30 minutes rather than drinking the cup at once.
items.cause: A hot liquid on a churning stomach can make things worse at first.

attrs.heading: If the cup did not help
